questions.text: I notice that changes in my environment — new sounds, new faces, new routines — don't produce much internal disturbance.
I use tools like spreadsheets, project boards, or apps to keep my life organized.
I find that my most productive periods are when I am singularly focused on my own objectives.
I notice when someone's smile fades the instant they think no one is watching.
I hold the view that finding shortcuts and faster paths is a form of intelligence.
I let group discussions find their own focus, even if they meander for a while.
I hold the view that objectivity in the face of others' hardship is a form of strength that benefits everyone involved.
I know my work is done when it feels right, not when it passes a formal checklist.
I believe indecision is more dangerous than a wrong decision.
I find that even minor social friction — an awkward pause, a misread joke — produces a noticeable spike of discomfort.
I notice when someone is telling a story primarily to position themselves in a certain light.
I tend to treat silence in a conversation as simply a pause rather than as communication.
I would work a job I dislike if it meant my family or community benefited greatly.
I often set deadlines and hold people to them.
I feel that my emotions operate on a hair trigger — it doesn't take much to set them off.
I find that the felt weight of another person's hardship is the factor that most reliably corrects my tendency toward self-centered decision-making.
I find that I am at my most effective when I can engage with a problem without being emotionally invested in the people involved.
I find that my mood is largely independent of external circumstances — it remains stable regardless.
I pick up on when someone is nodding along without actually agreeing.
What some call procrastination, I experience as waiting for the right moment of clarity.
I rarely pick up on when a host is ready for their guests to leave.
I tend to remain physically relaxed even when discussing difficult topics.
I feel a strong pull to shape the agenda of any group I am part of.
I tend to choose careers and projects based on how much they allow me to help others.
I establish clear criteria before evaluating options so my judgment stays consistent.
I wait to hear everyone's perspective before forming a group plan.
I believe personal achievement is one of the most fulfilling experiences in life.
I find that writing out my goals makes me significantly more likely to achieve them.
I feel that splurging on a personal passion project is money well spent.
I tend not to read meaning into someone choosing to communicate by text versus calling versus meeting in person.
I hold the view that the best outcomes come from shared decision-making.
I hold the view that the consistency of my moral concern for suffering — not its peak intensity — is the truest measure of its depth.
I believe that the consistency with which I take others' suffering seriously is the best indicator of whether my values are genuine.
My hands sometimes shake when I'm processing a strong emotion.
I think that the ability to evaluate situations of human suffering with precision requires deliberate emotional self-regulation.
I find it genuinely easier to give than to receive.
